coredata.dc:description: Superhydrophobic separation membranes are considered to be one of the most promising technologies for oil-water separation. However, the plastic waste generated from discarded membranes poses a challenge to the preparation of degraded superhydrophobic separation membranes for achieving eco-friendly separation. In this study, superhydrophobic poly(lactic acid) (PLA) membranes were fabricated using a non-solvent induced phase separation method assisted by l-cysteine modified carbon dots (Cys-CDs). The synergistic effect of Cys-CDs-induced crystallization behavior of PLA and the phase separation process results in the evolution of the surface of the PLA-based membrane from a pistil-like structure to a multi-level micro-nano structure composed of dense lamellar nanofibers and microspheres with an increase in Cys-CDs content. At a Cys-CDs content of 5 wt%, the surface roughness of PLA-based separation membrane reached its maximum, and the water contact angle was as high as 159°. Remarkably, the superhydrophobic Cys-CDs/PLA membrane exhibited promising performance in the separation of water-in-oil emulsions, with a rejection rate of 99.98% and a flux of 315.74 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1. Additionally, the superhydrophobic Cys-CDs/PLA separation membrane also demonstrates impressive properties such as acid-alkali resistance and rapid recycling into high-value chemicals. Consequently, this rapidly recoverable superhydrophobic porous Cys-CDs/PLA membrane shows great potential for practical applications in actual oil-water separation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Superhydrophobic separation membranes are considered to be one of the most promising technologies for oil-water separation. However, the plastic waste generated from discarded membranes poses a challenge to the preparation of degraded superhydrophobic separation membranes for achieving eco-friendly separation. In this study, superhydrophobic poly(lactic acid) (PLA) membranes were fabricated using a non-solvent induced phase separation method assisted by l-cysteine modified carbon dots (Cys-CDs). The synergistic effect of Cys-CDs-induced crystallization behavior of PLA and the phase separation process results in the evolution of the surface of the PLA-based membrane from a pistil-like structure to a multi-level micro-nano structure composed of dense lamellar nanofibers and microspheres with an increase in Cys-CDs content. At a Cys-CDs content of 5 wt%, the surface roughness of PLA-based separation membrane reached its maximum, and the water contact angle was as high as 159°. Remarkably, the superhydrophobic Cys-CDs/PLA membrane exhibited promising performance in the separation of water-in-oil emulsions, with a rejection rate of 99.98% and a flux of 315.74 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1. Additionally, the superhydrophobic Cys-CDs/PLA separation membrane also demonstrates impressive properties such as acid-alkali resistance and rapid recycling into high-value chemicals. Consequently, this rapidly recoverable superhydrophobic porous Cys-CDs/PLA membrane shows great potential for practical applications in actual oil-water separation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The discovery and utilization of oil have facilitated the rapid development of human industrial civilization, but they have also led to significant environmental pollution issues. In particular, the production of oily wastewater from industrial processes and marine oil spills has caused severe damage to the ecological environment and water resources security of the Earth [1,2]. Therefore, it is important to research and develop effective oil-water separation technologies and materials [3–5]. The commonly used technologies for oil-water separation include biodegradation [6], chemical flocculation [7], adsorption [8], and membrane separation [9]. Among these technologies, membrane separation technology is considered to be the most promising method for oil-water separation due to its advantages of simple operation, low energy consumption, high efficiency, wide applicability, and no secondary pollution [10].

In recent years, super-wetting oil-water separation membranes with superhydrophobicity have received extensive attention [11–13]. Compared to traditional oil-water separation membranes, the superhydrophobic membrane exhibits higher selectivity, separation efficiency and recyclability, providing broad prospects for the separation of oil-water mixtures and emulsions. Lin et al. [14] prepared the first artificial superhydrophobic-superoleophilic coating membrane by spraying polytetrafluoroethylene on a stainless steel mesh, which effectively separated the mixture of diesel oil and water. Inspired by this research, an increasing number of superhydrophobic membranes have been designed and fabricated. For instance, Wang et al. [15] manufactured a superhydrophobic wrinkled skin-covered polypropylene (PP) membrane through UV grafting and interfacial polymerization. This membrane exhibited excellent separation performance for oil-water mixtures and water-in-oil emulsions, with a retention rate of more than 99%. Another study conducted by Kang et al. [16] demonstrated that the water removal efficiency of the water-in-oil emulsions reached 98% when a superhydrophobic membrane, prepared by sequentially depositing polydopamine and poly(dimethylsiloxane) on fiberglass, was used. Zhao et al. [17] used the electrostatic spinning technique to fabricate a membrane of poly(vinylidene fluoride)-hexafluoropropylene/carbon nanotubes with superhydrophobic-superoleophilic properties, which showed a separation efficiency of over 99.5% for water-in-oil emulsions. Fan et al. [18] prepared a composite membrane of superhydrophobic-superoleophilic polyphenylene sulfide through thermally induced phase separation (TIPS), which exhibited lotus leaf-like micro-nano structures and could be used for oil/water separation in strongly acidic or alkaline environments, with a water rejection rate of emulsion exceeding 99.9%. The above studies show that superhydrophobic membranes do have excellent separation efficiency for oil-based oily wastewater. However, these membrane materials are usually non-biodegradable and prone to causing secondary environmental pollution upon disposal [19]. Therefore, it is of significant practical importance to develop biodegradable superhydrophobic separation membranes.

Poly(lactic acid) (PLA), one of the recognized biodegradable materials with good biocompatibility and chemical stability, is an ideal raw material for large-scale preparation of oil-water separation membranes [20]. However, the presence of a large number of hydrophilic groups in the PLA molecular chain makes it challenging to prepare superhydrophobic surfaces using PLA. Based on previous research, it has been found that achieving superhydrophobicity is feasible by surface modification or adding nanoparticles to assist in the construction of micro-nano rough structured surface [21]. A highly hydrophobic wood sponge coated with a layer of fluoroalkyl silane-modified reduced graphene oxide showed a high water contact angle of 145° and was successfully applied for separating oil-water mixtures, with a separation efficiency of 99.0% [22]. The construction of a superhydrophobic surface was achieved by growing and decorating multiscale zeolitic imidazolate frameworks on PLA nonwoven fabric, which exhibited high separation efficiency and recyclability in oil-water separation [23]. Additionally, a superhydrophobic PLA porous membrane for oil-water separation was fabricated based on fused deposition modeling three-dimensional printing and subsequent chemical etching and the decoration of polystyrene nanospheres [24]. Furthermore, the electrospun membrane prepared by Hao et al. [25], consisting of polydimethylsiloxane/nano-SiO2@PLA, has an oil-water separation efficiency of 99.9%. However, the preparation process of the above method is complicated, which makes the phase separation method with simple operation more attractive. Su et al. [26] prepared superhydrophobic stereo-complex PLA membranes with large oil absorption ability using the non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS) method and physically peeling off its skin layer. In the work of Xue Bai's research group [27], carbon quantum dots-grafted-poly(d-lactic acid)/poly(l-lactic acid) (CQDs-g-PDLA/PLLA) films were fabricated by incorporating 30% CQDs grafted with PDLA and nucleated interfacial-crystallization induced phase separation. The water contact angle (WCA) of the composite film was as high as 168°, and the maximum oil absorption rate was 9.7 g/g. Wang et al. [28] reported the fabrication of a superhydrophobic PLA with a honeycomb structure by the water-assisted TIPS method, which has an efficient oil-water separation ability. But regrettably, the superhydrophobic PLA membrane prepared using the NIPS method described in the existing literature is only effective in separating oil-water mixtures due to limitations in surface structure and pore size, making it unsuitable for efficient separation of water-in-oil emulsions.

The novelty of the present study lies in the use of a modified phase separation process to form superhydrophobic PLA composite membranes by properly controlling the crystallization and phase separation rate of PLA. In this study, weathered coal-based carbon dots (CDs) were amidated by L-cysteine (Cys) to enhance their compatibility with PLA. Then, a series of PLA-based composite hydrophobic porous membranes with varying contents of Cys-modified CDs (Cys-CDs) were prepared by the NIPS method [26]. The Cys-CDs/PLA membranes can effectively separate water-in-oil emulsions due to their rough micro-nano surface and porous internal structure. It is worth noting that Cys-CDs/PLA membrane containing 5 wt% Cys-CDs has remarkable superhydrophobicity, acid-alkali resistance, and rapid recyclability. This provides reliable support for the large-scale development and application of green oil-water separation membranes.

PLA (PLLA, pellet, injection grade, Mw = 100,000 g∙mol−1, ρ = 1.24 g∙cm−3) was purchased from Shenzhen Esun Industrial Co., Ltd., Shenzhen, China. Cys and 1,4-dithiothreitol (DTT) are analytical reagents and were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) are analytical reagents and were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., China. Copper sulfate solution (CuSO4, ≥99%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥99%), and hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%) were purchased from Tianjin Yongsheng Fine Chemicals Co., Ltd. Ethanol, dichloromethane (DCM), triethylamine (TEA), n-hexane and kerosene were used as analytical reagents, which were purchased from Tianjin Xinbote Chemical Co., Ltd. All the water used in the experiment was deionized. The chemicals and reagents used in this experiment were used as received without any purification.

The CDs were obtained from weathered coal through mild oxidation, and the detailed methods for their preparation and characterization are described elsewhere [29]. The ratio and conditions of amidation modification of CDs by Cys are based on previous research [29], with the only difference being that water is used as the reaction solvent, and a small amount of DTT is also added as a sulfhydryl protectant. The modification scheme of CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The Cys-CDs/PLA membranes were prepared by the NIPS method, and the content of Cys-CDs in the Cys-CDs/PLA commixture was set at 0, 1 wt%, 3 wt%, 5 wt%, and 7 wt%, respectively. It is important to note two key operational points for achieving a rough superhydrophobic PLA surface: firstly, the concentration of PLA dissolved in the solvent should be stable. Due to the high volatility of DCM, the dissolution of PLA can be carried out in a closed container at room temperature during winter, while the concentration of PLA in the solution can be stabilized by water bath during summer. Secondly, the rate of phase separation is controlled by slowly adding non-solvent at low temperatures to match the slow crystallization rate of PLA. In a typical procedure, as shown in Fig. 2 , a certain amount of Cys-CDs and 1 g PLA were added to 20 mL DCM solvent, ultrasonically dispersed for 1 min followed by magnetic stirring at room temperature for 2 h. Then 5 mL of the mixture was poured into a petri dish with a diameter of 6 mm placed on the ice, and an equal volume of C2H5OH was slowly dripped in. Subsequently, the petri dish containing the PLA solution was placed in a container containing saturated CuSO4 (98% relative humidity) for 6 h. Under saturated humidity, water vapor condenses into the phase interface between C2H5OH and DCM, reducing the phase separation rate and promoting crystal nucleation. Finally, the as-prepared Cys-CDs/PLA composite membrane was dried overnight in a fume hood to eliminate residual solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Density and porosity of Cys-CDs/PLA membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The density of the Cys-CDs/PLA membrane and its precursor, pure PLA, was measured by a multifunctional electronic densimeter (PPMD-A, Beijing Guangshi Jingdian Instruments & Equipment, China) using the water displacement method (ASTM D792). The porosity (P%) of the membranes was calculated by the simple density method [30].

where ρ is the density of the composite membrane and ρ s is the density of the pure PLA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oil sorption and oil-water separation tests of Cys-CDs/PLA membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
In a typical sorption experiment, the Cys-CDs/PLA membranes were separately immersed in an oil-water mixture stained with Sudan I (red) and methylene blue (blue) to achieve adsorption saturation. The mass before and after achieving adsorption saturation was recorded to calculate the adsorption capacity (Q, g/g) according to formula (2) [31].

where m1 (g) and m2 (g) represent the total mass of the membrane before and after oil absorption, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of water-in-oil emulsions separation performance of Cys-CDs/PLA membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
Two representative oil phases, n-hexane and kerosene, were selected to evaluate the water-in-oil emulsion separation performance of Cys-CDs/PLA membranes. The stable water-in-oil emulsions were prepared as follows: 1 mL of deionized water was added to 100 mL of oil, followed by the addition of 0.25 g of surfactant Span 80 for ultrasonic dispersion for 1 h, and then vigorous stirring for 5 h. The entire water-in-oil emulsion separation process was carried out in a dead-end filtration device, with 100 mL of emulsion filtered each time. Moreover, the effective filtration area of the membrane device was 13.202 cm2 and a transmembrane pressure of 0.1 bar was applied. The amount of filtrate was measured every 10 min to determine the real-time separation flux. The permeation flux (F, L·m−2·h−1·bar−1) [10] and rejection rate (R) can be measured according to the following formula:
(3)
F =
∆ V
A ∆ t ∆ P
(4)
R =
1 −
C 1
C 0
× 100 %
where ΔV (L) is the volume of the filtrate, A (m2) is the effective filtration area of the membrane device, Δt (h) is the running time corresponding to the volume of the filtrate, ΔP (bar) is the transmembrane pressure, C0 (L/L) is the initial water content of the emulsion, and C1 (L/L) is the water content of the filtrate. The initial water content of the emulsion and the water content of the filtrate were analyzed by the volumetric method on a Karl Fischer moisture meter (AKD-A9, Wuxi Cosari Instrumentation, China).

A complete composite membrane was placed in a 20 mL mixture of NaOH aqueous solution (10 g/L) and C2H5OH, with equal volumes, and the changes of the composite membrane over time were captured with a digital camera.

The changes in the chemical structure of CDs before and after modification were recorded by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Nicolet iS50, Thermo Scientific, USA) and a Thermo Fisher Scientific EscaLab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS). The water contact angle (WCA) of CDs and Cys-CDs was measured using a video optical contact angle meter (OCA 25, Data Physics, Germany). Similarly, measurements of WCA and water-slip angle (WSA) were conducted on Cys-CDs/PLA membranes with this instrument. The surface and cross-section morphology of the PLA-based membranes were observed by a Hitachi S-4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM). The surface roughness of the PLA-based membranes was determined by a Bruker Dimension Icon atomic force microscope (AFM), and the chemical groups on their surface were analyzed by attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy (Nicolet iS20, Thermo Scientific, USA). A Rigaku SmartLab SE X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD) and a TA Instruments Q2000 differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) were used to characterize the crystal structure and crystallinity of the PLA-based membranes. The crystallinity (Xc) of samples can be obtained by the following equation [32]:
(5)

1 − f
× 100 %
where ∆Hm represents the melting enthalpy of Cys-CDs/PLA membranes, ∆Hm 0 (93.7 J·g−1) is considered as the theoretical melting enthalpy for pure PLA, and f denotes the mass fraction of Cys-CDs. The particle size distribution and morphology of the emulsions were determined by a nanoparticle potentiometer (Nano ZS90, Malvern, England) and a digital optical microscope (XSP-BM-30 AD, China), respectively. The thermal stability of Cys-CDs/PLA membranes was measured on a HITACHI STA7300 thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA).

Since CDs are a water-soluble substance obtained by mild oxidation of weathered coal by H2O2/HCOOH [29], they have poor compatibility with PLA molecules. In this study, Cys was introduced onto the surface of CDs to improve its compatibility with PLA, and Cys-CDs were prepared through the one-pot reaction. The chemical structure of Cys-CDs was verified by FTIR and XPS, and its wettability was characterized by measuring WCA. As demonstrated in Fig. 3a, compared with the spectrum of CDs, the new appearance of vibration bands at 1625 cm−1 and 1578 cm−1 in the spectrum of Cys-CDs corresponded to the amide I and amide II bands, indicating that there was an amidation reaction between CDs and Cys to form NCO [33]. Cys-CDs showed three new peaks in the NH stretching vibration at 3036 cm−1, NH bending vibration at 1500 cm−1 and SH stretching vibration at 2080 cm−1, which are the characteristic absorption peaks of Cys [34]. The above analyses suggested that Cys was successfully grafted onto the surface of CDs.

The elemental composition and chemical structure of the Cys-CDs surface were further confirmed by XPS analysis. As illustrated in Fig. 3b, compared with CDs, the O content in Cys-CDs significantly decreased, while the N content increased and a new S element appeared. This indicates that Cys has an effective amidation modification on the surface of CDs, which is more clearly verified by the high-resolution XPS C1s spectra of CDs and Cys-CDs in Fig. 3c and d [33]. In other words, due to the amidation of carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs, the intensity of the CO peak in Cys-CDs is significantly lower than that in CDs. In addition, the type of chemical bonding for S can be seen from the high-resolution XPS spectrum of S2p (Fig. S1). The WCA of Cys-CDs (41.3°) was shown to be higher than that of CDs (27.7°) in Fig. 3b, which also indicated that the surface structure of CDs was successfully changed by Cys [35].

The surface morphology of the membrane is the key factor that affects its superhydrophobicity [36,37]. The evolution of surface morphology for the Cys-CDs/PLA membranes with different additions of Cys-CDs was observed by SEM. Fig. 4a depicted that the surface of the pure PLA membrane exhibited a single layer of clustered pistil-like structure, which was in sharp contrast to the multilayer structure of the PLA membrane added with Cys-CDs. At a Cys-CDs content of 1 wt%, the surface of the Cys-CDs/PLA membrane presented a two-layer micro-nano structure for the first time (Fig. 4b1–b3). The lower layer consists of a lamellar nanofiber structure, and the upper layer is composed of dispersed micron-sized clump with a rough lamellar structure. The two-level structure became more distinct as the Cys-CDs content increased to 3 wt% (Fig. 4c1–c3). The formation of the two-layer micro-nano structure arises from two different phase separation processes [27]. Cys-CDs act as effective heterogeneous nucleating agents for PLA [29]. The rapid liquid-solid phase separation triggered by heterogeneous nucleation crystallization produces numerous crystallites, and then a slow liquid-liquid phase separation occurs around crystallites induced by slower homogeneous crystallization of PLA. When incorporated with 5 wt% Cys-CDs, the lamellar nanofiber structure in the lower layer became smaller and denser, while the upper layer evolved into a large number of rough microsphere structures, resulting in the evolution of the composite membrane surface into a multi-level micro-nano structure (Fig. 4d1–d3). This is due to the enhanced heterogeneous nucleation of Cys-CDs, which can produce more and smaller crystallites more quickly in a limited space [36]. This enhancement is more obvious with the increasing contents of Cys-CDs into 7 wt%, and it could be clearly observed that smaller and more microspheres accumulated on the surface of the composite membrane, as indicated in Fig. 4e1–e3. The morphology of the micro-nano structure directly affects the surface roughness of the composite membrane. The analysis of the surface roughness of the composite membranes by AFM showed that the root-mean-square roughness (Rq) of pure PLA with a pistil-like structure was the lowest (Fig. 4a4), and the Rq of the micro-nano structure increased with an increase in Cys-CDs concentration (Fig. 4a4 and c4). When the Cys-CDs concentration was 5 wt%, the highest surface roughness was observed on the multi-level micro-nano structure, and its Rq value was as high as 114.0 nm (Fig. 4d4). Continuing to increase the concentration of Cys-CDs will result in a decrease in surface roughness, leading to a reduction of the Rq value to 101.0 nm (Fig. 4e4). This may be attributed to the fact that pure PLA can only undergo homogeneous crystallization during the phase separation process, resulting in slow crystallinity and a low rate of crystallization [36]. As heterogeneous nucleating agents, Cys-CDs can improve the crystallinity and crystallization rate of PLA [29], and with the increase of the addition amount, they can effectively promote the regular arrangement and growth of PLA molecular chains, thereby enhancing the roughness of the membranes. However, considering that the total amount of PLA molecular chains remains constant, an increase in the content of Cys-CDs leads to a higher number of nucleation centers, which results in a decrease in the PLA molecular chains involved in the growth of each nucleating agent [27]. Consequently, smaller and denser crystals will be formed, which will lead to a decrease in roughness. Surface roughness is an important factor affecting the hydrophobicity of the membrane [38]. Since neither PLA nor Cys-CDs possess superhydrophobic structures, the key to preparing superhydrophobic composite membranes lies in constructing a suitable micro-nano structure by adjusting the content of Cys-CDs to form a rough surface.

The NIPS method for preparing Cys-CDs/PLA membranes is based on the principle of slow replacement between the solvent (DCM, 1.325 g/cm3) and the non-solvent (C2H5OH, 0.7893 g/cm3), which induces a gradual phase separation between the solute (PLA) and the solvent to form a porous membrane. The different displacement rates of solvent and non-solvent on the surface and inside of the solution lead to a significant disparity in the surface and internal structure of the membrane [39]. The SEM images (Fig. 5 ) illustrate that all Cys-CDs/PLA membranes have obvious boundaries in their cross-sections. The side view of the thinner surface reveals a dense coral-like structure, indicating that the surface is rough, while the internal structure of the membrane below the surface exhibits a loose porous structure, mainly with a pore size of about 20 μm. The internal pores of the pure PLA membranes are irregular in shape due to extrusion or collapse, while the internal pore shapes of composite membranes are more regular, and their average statistical pore sizes decrease with an increase in Cys-CDs content. This is mainly due to the fact that Cys-CDs promote faster crystallization of PLA after the liquid-liquid phase separation process, which provides greater support for the PLA framework [39]. The stable porous structure offers a solid foundation for efficient oil-water separation.

The formation of micro-nano structures on the membrane surface is closely related to the crystallization behavior of PLA [30]. To further confirm the heterogeneous nucleation of Cys-CDs, the membranes were characterized by XPS, DSC, XRD, and ATR-FTIR. As shown in Fig. S2, the content of elements N and S in the Cys-CDs/PLA membranes gradually increases with an increasing proportion of Cys-CDs. This is because it can be discerned from the XPS analysis in the preceding section that the N and S elements primarily originate from Cys-CDs. As the incorporation amount of Cys-CDs increases, the contents of N and S elements in the Cys-CDs/PLA membranes correspondingly rise. Fig. 6a reveals that pure PLA shows a sharp endothermic peak at 168.5 °C, and its crystallinity is only 38.25%. As expected, the crystallinity initially increases and then decreases with the increasing content of Cys-CDs. Specifically, the crystallinity increases from 39.48% for 1 wt% Cys-CDs to 42.75% for 3 wt% Cys-CDs and reaches 47.86% for 5 wt% Cys-CDs, indicating a significant heterogeneous nucleation effect of Cys-CDs on PLA [40]. However, while being involved in a higher content of 7 wt% Cys-CDs, the crystallinity of the Cys-CDs/PLA membrane slightly decreases. This decrease may be attributed to the small-scale agglomeration of excess Cys-CDs particles [41], which is similar to a phenomenon observed in previous research [29]. Remarkably, the melting temperatures of Cys-CDs/PLA membranes with different Cys-CDs contents were all around 167 °C, suggesting that a stable α-crystalline phase was formed through either Cys-CDs-induced heterogeneous nucleation crystallization or its homogeneous nucleation crystallization [42]. Fig. 6b presents the XRD patterns of the porous membrane with different Cys-CDs contents. The same diffraction peaks were observed from all the PLA-based porous membranes at 12.5°, 15.1°, 16.8°, 19.0°, and 22.5°, corresponding to the (103), (010) (200/110), (203), and (210) planes of the α phase, respectively [43], which was consistent with the DSC results. Changes in PLA crystallinity are also reflected in the ATR-FTIR spectra [44]. As illustrated in Fig. 6c, the absorption peak at 1760 cm−1 and the combined band absorption peak at 1210–1180 cm−1 are both characteristic absorption peaks of crystalline PLA, corresponding to the stretching vibration peaks of CO and OCO, respectively. The intensities of these peaks reflect the content of crystalline PLA [44,45]. The trends of intensity changes in these two absorption peaks are clearly consistent with the DSC results. In addition, as a heterogeneous nucleating agent, Cys-CDs can induce the orderly crystallization of PLA on its surface during the crystallization process, which makes it difficult to observe the characteristic absorption peak of Cys-CDs in the surface ATR-FTIR spectra of Cys-CDs/PLA membranes. This phenomenon is similar to that observed in the work of C. Mortalò et al. [46].

The results of the above analyses indicate that the addition of Cys-CDs can effectively regulate the crystallization behavior of PLA, resulting in a composite membrane with a rough micro-nano structured surface and a loose porous internal skeleton. This unique combination structure is the result of the synergistic effect between the crystallization behavior of PLA and the phase separation process of the NIPS method. Based on the assumption that the liquid-liquid phase separation rate is much higher than the crystallization rate [36,47], and that the homogeneous nucleation rate of PLA is lower than the heterogeneous nucleation rate [29], the possible formation process of the combination structure is modeled in Fig. 6d. When non-solvent (C2H5OH) is slowly added to the DCM solution containing dissolved PLA molecules, the concentration difference between the solvent and non-solvent induces liquid-liquid phase separation, which gradually leads to the formation of polymer-rich and polymer-lean phase [27,36,47]. The diffusion resistance of C2H5OH into the interior of the solution is large due to its much smaller density compared to that of DCM. As a result, the polymer-lean phase in the interior of the PLA solution is much less than that on the surface of the PLA solution and can only exist as a dispersed phase. The volatility of DCM is much higher than that of C2H5OH. Therefore, as DCM further volatilizes and C2H5OH diffuses, the polymer-lean phase on the surface of the solution will gradually transform into a continuous phase, resulting in a gradual increase in the concentration of PLA molecules in the polymer-rich phase (continuous phase) within the solution. Due to the slow rate of homogeneous crystallization of PLA molecules, only a small amount of PLA molecules in the pure PLA solution are arranged orderly and homogeneously crystallized, while most of the PLA molecules reach the precipitation concentration but cannot be arranged orderly within a limited time, resulting in the formation of an unstable skeletal structure. As an effective heterogeneous nucleating agent, the addition of Cys-CDs increases the crystallization rate of PLA and enhances the proportion of crystalline PLA molecules, thereby obtaining a more stable skeletal structure. The polymer-lean phase is isolated by the PLA skeleton structure, and the pores are formed through the volatilization of C2H5OH. As a result, in the interior of the solution, the polymer-rich phase forms the skeletal structure while the polymer-lean phase forms the large pores, which is in agreement with the analysis of Sun et al. [36]. On the surface of the solution, the polymer-rich phase becomes a dispersed phase, and its volume gradually shrinks to form a large number of concentration points for molecular chains, which can serve as the crystal nucleus of PLA crystallization and induce orderly arrangement of PLA molecules with strong mobility on their surface in the polymer-lean phase [30,48]. The condensation of water vapor and the low volatility of C2H5OH prolonged the growth time of PLA crystals, facilitating the formation of regular structures. The heterogeneous nucleation crystallization induced by Cys-CDs competes with the homogeneous nucleation crystallization of PLA, and the limited number of PLA molecules in the polymer-lean phase is distributed around more crystallites, which may be the reason for the evolution of the micro-nano structure on the surface of Cys-CDs/PLA composite membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wettability and oil sorption properties of Cys-CDs/PLA membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The wettability of the Cys-CDs/PLA membrane is mainly affected by its surface roughness [36]. In this work, we improved the surface roughness of PLA membranes by incorporating different contents of Cys-CDs. Fig. 7a illustrates that the WCA of pure PLA membrane was only 122°. With an increase in Cys-CDs content, the WCA gradually increased until it reached a maximum value of 159° at a Cys-CDs content of 5 wt%, which meets one of the indicators of superhydrophobicity. This result is closely associated with the multi-level micro-nano structure of the membrane surface. With the increasing content of Cys-CDs into 7 wt%, the WCA decreased to 151°, which can be ascribed to the reduction in membrane surface roughness at this point. WSA serves as another indicator for defining superhydrophobic materials [27]. The WSA test was performed on the three membranes with higher WCA, as shown in Fig. 7b. When the content of Cys-CDs was 5 wt% and 7 wt%, the WSA of both membranes was less than 10°, and water droplets easily slid on them (Fig. 7f, Supplementary Video 1 and Supplementary Video 2), indicating that they were superhydrophobic membranes. Considering the practical applications of superhydrophobic membranes, their superhydrophobic stability under different droplets was investigated. Fig. 7d presents that water droplets containing strong acid (pH = 1, methyl orange staining), strong alkali (pH = 14, phenolphthalein staining), salt, methylene blue dye, and coffee could maintain their droplet shape on the surface of the membrane, indicating that the Cys-CDs/PLA membrane exhibits highly stable superhydrophobicity [49]. Furthermore, Fig. S4 illustrates the performance of Cys-CDs/PLA (5 wt%) membrane in the separation of water-in-kerosene and water-in-hexane surfactant-stabilized emulsions under strongly acidic (pH = 1) and strongly alkaline (pH = 14) environment. The membrane exhibits an excellent separation efficiency (>99.9%) in both acidic and alkaline emulsions, indicating the acid and alkali resistance of the superhydrophobic membrane. Conversely, oil droplets (Sudan I staining) were instantly absorbed upon contact with the surface of the membrane (Supplementary Video 3), where the Cys-CDs/PLA membrane showed superhydrophobicity. Therefore, the Cys-CDs/PLA membranes can be used for oil-water separation, and the oil-water separation performance of Cys-CDs/PLA membranes was evaluated by oil-water separation experiments. As seen in Fig. 7e, after the membrane was placed in the oil-water mixture solution, it could rapidly adsorb kerosene (Sudan I staining) on the water surface (methylene blue staining) without absorbing water. The Cys-CDs/PLA membranes with different Cys-CDs contents exhibited varying adsorption capacities for kerosene and n-hexane (Fig. 7c). With the increase in Cys-CDs content, the adsorption capacities of the membranes for kerosene and n-hexane showed a similar trend, which was essentially consistent with the change in membrane porosity (Fig. S2). Moreover, the Cys-CDs/PLA membranes also exhibited good oil-water separation performance in the filtration device (Fig. S3, Supplementary Video 4). The effect of different Cys-CDs contents on the mechanical properties of Cys-CDs/PLA membranes was examined in Fig. S5. The elongation at break increases as the Cys-CDs content rises, reaching the maximum at a Cys-CDs concentration of 5 wt%, and then decreases as Cys-CDs content further increases. These findings are consistent with the results obtained from the DSC analysis of Cys-CDs/PLA membranes. However, the tensile strength shows minor changes in response to variations in Cys-CDs content. When the concentration of Cys-CDs is not more than 5 wt%, the uniform dispersion of small-sized Cys-CDs within the membrane matrix and their strong interaction facilitate the effective tension transfer from the membrane matrix to the high strength Cys-CDs, achieving good stress dispersion [29]. Conversely, an excessive amount of Cys-CDs particles becomes a stress concentration point and reduces the toughness of the membrane [29]. Therefore, the incorporation of Cys-CDs can enhance the mechanical properties of the Cys-CDs/PLA membrane to a certain extent. All these results indicate that the Cys-CDs/PLA superhydrophobic membranes have great potential for applications in oil-water separation.

The wettability of the Cys-CDs/PLA membrane is mainly affected by its surface roughness [36]. In this work, we improved the surface roughness of PLA membranes by incorporating different contents of Cys-CDs. Fig. 7a illustrates that the WCA of pure PLA membrane was only 122°. With an increase in Cys-CDs content, the WCA gradually increased until it reached a maximum value of 159° at a Cys-CDs content of 5 wt%, which meets one of the indicators of superhydrophobicity. This result is closely associated with the multi-level micro-nano structure of the membrane surface. The membrane exhibits an excellent separation efficiency (>99.9%) in both acidic and alkaline emulsions, indicating the acid and alkali resistance of the superhydrophobic membrane. As seen in Fig. 7e, after the membrane was placed in the oil-water mixture solution, it could rapidly adsorb kerosene (Sudan I staining) on the water surface (methylene blue staining) without absorbing water. The Cys-CDs/PLA membranes with different Cys-CDs contents exhibited varying adsorption capacities for kerosene and n-hexane (Fig. 7c). All these results indicate that the Cys-CDs/PLA superhydrophobic membranes have great potential for applications in oil-water separation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emulsion separation performance of Cys-CDs/PLA membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Two water-in-oil emulsions (n-hexane and kerosene) were selected to evaluate the emulsion separation performance of the Cys-CDs/PLA membranes on a dead-end filtration separation device (Fig. S3). Fig. 8a presents a diagram illustrating the rejection rate of the Cys-CDs/PLA membranes for the separation of water-in-n-hexane and water-in-kerosene emulsions. The results demonstrated that with the increase in Cys-CDs content, the rejection rate of the composite membrane for both water-in-oil emulsions increased, especially for the kerosene emulsion, which increased from 73.29% of pure PLA to 99.98%. This is also visually verified by the optical images of the filtrate (Fig. 8d). In general, there is an inverse relationship between the rejection rate and flux. As observed in Fig. 8b, the flux of the composite membrane towards the two water-in-oil emulsions initially decreased and then slightly increased with the increase in Cys-CDs content, which was consistent with the change in the wettability of the membrane surface. It is possibly because the Cys-CDs/PLA membranes cannot be wetted by water droplets, and there is a repulsive force that can prevent water droplets from passing through the membranes [19]. Due to the upward and backward repulsion force, it can overcome a certain number of driving force (gravity) [3]. Moreover, this repulsive force is proportional to the surface roughness [19], so the greater the surface roughness, the higher the mass transfer resistance, resulting in a smaller flux. The flux of pure PLA membrane to n-hexane emulsion was the highest, with a value of 756.28 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1, while the flux of Cys-CDs/PLA (5 wt%) membrane to the kerosene emulsion was the lowest at 315.74 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1. The difference in the rejection rate and flux between n-hexane and kerosene is mainly due to the different sizes of water droplets and oil molecules within the emulsion. As presented in Fig. 5c, under the same preparation conditions, the diameter of all water droplets in n-hexane emulsion is above 100 nm, while about half of the water droplets in kerosene emulsion have a diameter ranging from 10 to 100 nm. It is well known that the average molecular weight of kerosene is in the range of 200–250 g/mol, while n-hexane has a molecular weight of 86 g/mol. Therefore, on the same micro-nano structure surface, water droplets with larger diameters are more likely to be intercepted, whereas oil molecules with smaller molecular weights are easier to pass through. To gain a more comprehensive understanding of the impressive separation ability of the Cys-CDs/PLA (5 wt%) superhydrophobic membrane, we utilized an optical microscope to observe the two emulsions before and after separation. The optical images (Fig. 8e) displayed distinct circles (water droplets) in the n-hexane and kerosene emulsions before separation. However, no discernible circles were captured in the images after separation, which further verifies the efficient emulsion separation efficiency of the superhydrophobic membrane. After 5 cycles, the membrane still maintains a high separation efficiency (Fig. 8g). The slight decrease in the emulsion flux (Fig. 8h) of the membrane is possibly due to the blockage of its internal pores by adsorbed oil droplets. Additionally, Fig. 8i demonstrates that the superhydrophobic separation membrane also possesses self-cleaning capability. The principle is that the aqueous solution containing pollutants cannot penetrate the interior of the Cys-CDs/PLA (5 wt%) superhydrophobic membrane when it comes into contact with the multi-level rough surface. An air cushion is simply created beneath the water droplet, assisted by the entrapped air in micro-nano structures on the surface of superhydrophobic PLA membrane, which contributes to a significant decrease in the contact area between PLA surface and the water droplet [50]. Predictably, the water droplet only has a point contact with the surface of the PLA-based membrane and easily slides off the surface to achieve self-cleaning capability. Recent representative works on superhydrophobic membranes for emulsion separation are summarized in Table 1 . It can be readily observed that the hydrophobicity and separation efficiency of the Cys-CDs/PLA superhydrophobic membrane, as explored in this investigation, rival those of other superhydrophobic membranes documented in previous studies. Moreover, the raw materials employed in this study exude more green and biodegradable properties, and the experimental protocol itself espouses an inclination towards simplicity and energy conservation. The above analyses indicate that the superhydrophobic PLA composite membranes with a Cys-CDs content of 5 wt% and 7 wt% can efficiently separate water-in-oil emulsions and have potential applications in industrial separation processes.

Based on the above analyses, we propose a reasonable mechanism for emulsion separation (Fig. 8f). When the water-in-oil emulsion comes into contact with the surface of the Cys-CDs/PLA membrane, the oil phase rapidly penetrates its multi-level micro-nano structure and is adsorbed by the membrane until saturation is reached. The adsorbed oil, together with the PLA composite membrane, forms a barrier against water droplets, thereby further enhancing the hydrophobicity of the membrane. When water droplets of different sizes reach the surface of the membrane, the water droplets with larger diameters are intercepted on the surface through the screening effect of the multi-level micro-nano structure surface [3,11]. Meanwhile, due to the downward driving force, water droplets with smaller diameters slide rapidly on the oil surface in the multi-level micro-nano structure, resulting in coalescence and demulsification, thus forming larger water droplets that are intercepted [59]. In contrast, oil can smoothly traverse the surface of the adsorption saturation membrane and quickly pass through the oil passage (internal pores), achieving efficient separation of the emulsion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability and recyclability of Cys-CDs/PLA membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal stability and recyclability of Cys-CDs/PLA membrane are of great significance, as they not only determine the temperature range for membrane usage but also affect resource sustainability and environmental preservation. The TGA and DTG curves of the Cys-CDs/PLA membranes are shown in Fig. 9a and b, respectively, and the relevant pyrolysis characteristic values are listed in Table 2 . The pyrolysis curve of pure PLA membrane exhibits a fast pyrolysis stage, and as the content of Cys-CDs increases, the pyrolysis curve of the composite membranes gradually evolves into two distinct fast pyrolysis stages. The degree of pyrolysis in the new fast pyrolysis stage is proportional to the amount of Cys-CDs added, indicating that Cys-CDs are uniformly dispersed within the PLA membrane. The addition of Cys-CDs results in a reduction in both the initial and maximum thermal decomposition temperature of PLA, with a maximum difference of 14.4 °C, which may be attributed to the thermal decomposition of Cys-CDs. In principle, the thermal stability of PLA increases with an increase of crystallinity [36], but the low thermal stability of Cys-CDs leads to a decrease in the overall thermal stability of Cys-CDs/PLA membranes, which needs to be improved in future research.

As is known to all, PLA is one of the biodegradable materials, but its degradation process is slow and affected by many factors. Moreover, a large amount of biodegradation can also lead to resource waste and an increase in the greenhouse effect [60,61]. Therefore, it is of great practical importance to investigate the recyclability of Cys-CDs/PLA membrane. As shown in Fig. 9c, the Cys-CDs/PLA membrane decomposed rapidly at room temperature in a solution of NaOH aqueous solution (10 g/L) and C2H5OH mixed at a volume ratio of 1:1. After being immersed in the solution for only 1 min, it broke upon touch and could not be picked up with tweezers. With the prolongation of time, the entire membrane gradually disintegrated into small pieces and particles until it completely disappeared. The whole process took about 90 min. This may be due to the loose porous structure of the Cys-CDs/PLA membrane, which allows sufficient contact between the solution, resulting in an alcoholysis reaction and the production of valuable ethyl lactate [61,62].

In summary, Cys-CDs were synthesized by amidating the surface of CDs under mild conditions to enhance their interfacial compatibility with PLA. Then, Cys-CDs/PLA porous composite membranes were prepared using the NIPS method with Cys-CDs as fillers. With the increase of Cys-CDs content, the surface morphology of the composite membrane evolved from a pistil-like structure to a multi-level micro-nano structure composed of dense lamellar nanofibers and microspheres. The formation of multi-level micro-nano structure endows the Cys-CDs/PLA porous membranes with superhydrophobic/superoleophilic wettability, which can be used for effective gravity-driven separation of immiscible oil-water mixtures and efficient separation of water-in-oil emulsions (rejection rate up to 99.98%). Furthermore, this superhydrophobic membrane also demonstrates a certain level of toughness and resistance to acid and alkali, making it suitable for large-scale application in the treatment of actual oily wastewater. It is noteworthy that Cys-CDs/PLA porous membranes can be used to recover valuable chemicals through rapid alcoholysis, offering new insights into the green and sustainable development of PLA.
